metadata.title: Barrios Visibles | Argentina
metadata.description: Explore informal settlements in Argentina and compare official RENABAP data with satellite-derived building footprints.

stats.placeholder: Zoom to a settlement to view detailed statistics.

about.description: Barrios Visibles compares official census data from RENABAP with satellite-derived building footprints to reveal gaps in how Argentina counts its informal settlements.
about.credits: Built with data from Google-Microsoft-OSM Open Buildings and RENABAP.

step1.title: Global data, local impact
step1.description:
Census data age rapidly. Argentina's official records haven't been updated since 2023—but informal settlements grow every day.

What if satellite imagery could fill the gap?

step2.title: La Plata: The case study
step2.conclusion: That's 41,575 buildings unaccounted for—representing 137,000–229,000 people missing from official records.

step3.title: Barrio Sin Nombre: 12x undercount
step3.conclusion: Official data captures less than 10% of the structures in this single settlement.

step4.title: This pattern repeats nationwide
step4.conclusion: If the La Plata pattern holds, over a million families may be missing from Argentina's official records.

legend.info: This gradient shows the discrepancy between official RENABAP data and satellite-detected building footprints. Amber indicates areas where buildings significantly outnumber official records.

explainer.title: About This Project
explainer.intro: Argentina's official registry of informal settlements undercounts by roughly 83%. This map compares what the government records against what satellites can see.

problem.title: The Problem
problem.p1: RENABAP, Argentina's official registry of barrios populares, lists 1.24 million families across 6,467 settlements. But satellite imagery reveals 1.97 million buildings within those same boundaries—59% more structures than recorded families.
problem.p2: This isn't about the registry being outdated. RENABAP's own quality-control protocol requires that family counts match dwellings visible in satellite imagery. The gap documented here is a departure from that standard. Closing it requires methodological change, not just updated data.

findings.title: What the Data Shows
findings.stat1: Official: 1.24M families. Building-based floor: 2.27M households. The registry undercounts by 83%.
findings.stat2: 2.9 to 3.4 million people unaccounted for—roughly 6 to 7% of Argentina's total population.
findings.stat3: Estimated informal-settlement population: 6.3 to 7.6 million, vs. official 3.5 to 4.1 million.
findings.source: Based on spatial analysis of 6,467 registered settlements.

methodology.title: How This Was Measured
methodology.p1: For each settlement, we establish a household floor: the maximum of (a) RENABAP's recorded families or (b) building footprints scaled by RENABAP's own 1.1 families-per-dwelling ratio. This captures undercount regardless of whether it comes from too few buildings counted or too few families per building.
methodology.p2: The pattern is geographically bimodal. In Buenos Aires city (CABA), consolidated multi-story villas mean RENABAP captures more families than footprints suggest (median 1.9 families per building).
methodology.p2cont: Everywhere else—90% of settlements—the pattern reverses: building counts substantially exceed family estimates. This matches systematic undercount patterns documented globally in informal settlements.

caveats.title: Important Limitations
caveats.item1: These are floor estimates, not ceilings. In vertically-densified settlements, actual households exceed building counts. The true population is likely higher.
caveats.item2: RENABAP only registers settlements in localities above 2,000 inhabitants. Settlements in smaller localities are missing from both the registry and this analysis.
caveats.item3: This analysis has not been formally ground-truthed. It is intended to motivate field enumeration, not substitute for it.

why.title: Why This Matters
why.p1: RENABAP serves as the de facto population basis for infrastructure investment, flood risk planning, and service delivery across Argentina. When it undercounts by 83%, planning decisions rest on a false foundation.
why.p2: In La Plata, where this research began, the municipality has now incorporated building footprint data into their first comprehensive plan. The methodology is simple, reproducible, and runs in seconds using open data.

footnotes.title: Sources & References
footnotes.fn1: Evaluating gridded population estimates in slums: none of 9 products captured >39% of field-enumerated residents.
footnotes.fn2: RENABAP methodology manual: family counts derive from referente declarations, field counts, and satellite imagery.
footnotes.fn3: Do we underestimate slum dwellers? Systematic undercount patterns in Global South cities.
footnotes.fn4: High-resolution population estimation using household survey data and building footprints.
footnotes.fn5: Household size multipliers: 2.8 (INDEC national average) and 3.35 (RENABAP barrios populares).
footnotes.fn6: Full working paper with methodology, sensitivity analysis, and province-level breakdown.

data.title: Data Sources
data.intro: This analysis compares RENABAP's official enumeration against satellite-derived building footprints within registered settlement boundaries.

buildings.title: Building Footprints
buildings.description: The VIDA combined dataset merges building footprints from Google Open Buildings, Microsoft GlobalMLFootprints, and OpenStreetMap. Within RENABAP settlement polygons, 1.97 million footprints are detected—59% more than the 1.24 million families officially recorded.

renabap.title: RENABAP
renabap.description: The Registro Nacional de Barrios Populares is Argentina's official registry of informal settlements, maintained by the Secretaría de Integración Socio-Urbana. It documents 6,467 barrios populares with 1.24 million families. Family counts derive from referente declarations, field counts, and satellite dwelling counts, multiplied by 1.1 families per dwelling.

technical.title: Technical Details
technical.item1: Spatial join: buildings intersecting settlement polygons via DuckDB
technical.item2: Building data: VIDA combined dataset via Source.Cooperative
technical.item3: Urban classification: IGN Planta Urbana layer
technical.item4: Full code and data available; analysis reproducible in ~3 seconds
